perturbation.original_text: Additionally, t he fact of the Parties discussions regarding a p otential business relationship shall be deemed Confidential Information subject to the protections of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Additionally, the fact of the Parties' discussions regarding a potential business relationship may be considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall be deemed Confidential Information' to 'may be considered Confidential Information' introduces ambiguity. It creates an in-text contradiction because while the agreement aims to protect confidential information, this clause now makes the confidentiality of the discussions optional, creating uncertainty about what is truly protected under the agreement. This affects the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions, allowing the Receiving Party to potentially disclose details of the discussions, since these may not be 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Disclosure and Protection of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party shall (i) hold all Confidential Information in confidence, and shall not disclose Confidential Information except as expressly permitted under the terms of this Agreement and (ii) use Confidential Information only for the NDA Purpose and for no other purpose without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that discussions 'shall be deemed Confidential Information', which imposes a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'may be considered Confidential Information', making confidentiality optional. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states 'The Receiving Party shall (i) hold all Confidential Information in confidence', creating a strong in-text contradiction. The changed text introduces ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions outlined in the contradicted text, aligning with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall (i) hold all Confidential Information in confidence, and shall not disclose Confidential Information except as expressly permitted under the terms of this Agreement and (ii) use Confidential Information only for the NDA Purpose and for no other purpose without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall use Confidential Information only for the NDA Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit requirement to 'hold all Confidential Information in confidence' and the prohibition on disclosure, the altered text creates an in-text contradiction. Other sections still refer to 'Confidential Information,' assuming it is protected, but this central obligation is now missing. This impacts the fundamental purpose of the NDA, making it unclear what protections are actually in place and therefore creating uncertainty. If the receiving party releases the information it is unclear based on this change what is a violation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Scope and Term of Agreement. This Agreement pertains to all Confidential Information that is disclosed o r otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates to the Receiving Party in connection with the NDA Purpose, whether prior to or during the term of this Agreement. The term of this Agreement shall commence as of the Effective Date set f orth below and shall continue for a period of twelve (12) months thereafter.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the Receiving Party must hold Confidential Information in confidence and not disclose it except as permitted. The changed text removes this explicit protection, only requiring the use of Confidential Information for the NDA Purpose. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which assumes that 'all Confidential Information' is protected by the Agreement. The changed text makes it unclear whether disclosure is prohibited, weakening the agreement and creating a direct conflict regarding the scope of protection for Confidential Information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement pertains to all Confidential Information that is disclosed o r otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates to the Receiving Party in connection with the NDA Purpose, whether prior to or during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement pertains to information disclosed in connection with the NDA Purpose, whether prior to or during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Confidential Information' from this phrase creates an in-text contradiction. The rest of the agreement still references 'Confidential Information' and assumes that Section 5 clearly defines what the agreement covers. By removing this term here the agreement now refers to 'information' without specifying the protection, it contradicts the understanding that disclosed information is to be 'Confidential'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Return of Documents. The Receiving Party shall, upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, return to the Disclos ing Party or, at the Disclosing Party’s direction, destroy all documents and any and all other tangible manifestations of Confidential Information received or created by the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions there of).
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly refers to 'Confidential Information' which is disclosed, tying it to the NDA's purpose. The changed text removes the term 'Confidential Information', broadening the scope to simply 'information'. This directly contradicts the 'Return of Documents' clause, which specifically requires the return or destruction of 'Confidential Information'. The modified text creates a contradiction because the Receiving Party is now obligated to return only 'Confidential Information' as defined elsewhere, but the agreement's scope has broadened to include all 'information' disclosed. This makes the scope of return unclear and creates a strong in-text contradiction as outlined in Perturbation Explanation and Omissions – In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall not apply with respect to any Confidential Information to the extent the Receiving Party can demonstrate that such Confidential Information: (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is in the Receiving Party’s rightful possession at the time of disclosure without an obligation to hold it in Page 2 of 2 confidence; (c) becomes known to the Receiving Party without obligation of confidence through disclosure by a third party h aving the legal right to disclose such information; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without access or reference to, or reliance upon, the Confidential Information ; or (e) is or becomes disclosed through the ordinary course of the NDA Purpose or as an element of any marketing plan intended to be released in the public domain or to targeted geographic public domain .
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Information shall not apply to information that: (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure; (b) is in the Receiving Party’s rightful possession at the time of disclosure; (c) becomes known to the Receiving Party without obligation of confidence through disclosure by a third party; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party; or (e) is or becomes disclosed through the ordinary course of the NDA Purpose or as an element of any marketing plan intended to be released in the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of 'Confidential' before 'Information' in the introductory phrase and in clauses (a), (d), and using only 'information' results in an in-text contradiction. The initial statement refers to 'Confidential Information,' but the exception clauses now only refer to 'Information', and removes the explicit mention of 'breach of this agreement', suggesting that the exceptions apply to all types of information, not just confidential information. This contradicts the purpose of an NDA which is to protect confidential information not all information. Also removing the explicit mention of 'breach of this agreement', suggests that the exceptions apply to all types of information, not just confidential information
perturbation.contradicted_text: Limitation on Obligations. The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Information shall not apply to information that: (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure; (b) is in the Receiving Party’s rightful possession at the time of disclosure; (c) becomes known to the Receiving Party without obligation of confidence through disclosure by a third party; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party; or (e) is or becomes disclosed through the ordinary course of the NDA Purpose or as an element of any marketing plan intended to be released in the public domain.
perturbation.justification: The original text and the contradicted text both define exceptions to the Receiving Party's obligations concerning the Disclosing Party's 'Confidential Information'. The changed text, however, omits the word 'Confidential' in the introductory phrase and within some of the exception clauses, broadening the scope of the exceptions to include 'Information' in general. This creates a contradiction because the NDA is specifically designed to protect confidential information, not all information. The changed text weakens the protection of confidential information by potentially allowing exceptions for any type of information, conflicting with the NDA's core purpose and creating an in-text contradiction as defined by 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Return of Documents. The Receiving Party shall, upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, return to the Disclos ing Party or, at the Disclosing Party’s direction, destroy all documents and any and all other tangible manifestations of Confidential Information received or created by the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions there of).
perturbation.changed_text: Return of Documents. The Receiving Party shall, upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, return to the Disclosing Party or destroy documents related to the Parties' discussions.
perturbation.explanation: The change from returning 'all tangible manifestations of Confidential Information' to returning 'documents related to the Parties' discussions' introduces an in-text contradiction. By narrowing the scope of returned documents, the revised text contradicts the broader definition of 'Confidential Information' established (however weakened) in earlier sections. It creates uncertainty about whether other forms of confidential information (e.g., electronic data, prototypes) must also be returned and therefore reduces the enforceability of the obligation to return confidential data and documents.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information. As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean information designated as confidential by the Disclosing Party. By way of example, Confidential Information includes, without limitation, information regarding a Party’s products, product development, services, agreements and contracts, business operations, business plans and financial affairs; employees and other personnel; vendors and customers; marketing plans and strategies; processes, compensation formulas, methods and specifications; and information technology network.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the return of 'all tangible manifestations of Confidential Information,' which aligns with the broad definition of 'Confidential Information' in the contradicted text (including information regarding products, business plans, employee data, etc.). The changed text narrows the scope to only 'documents related to the Parties' discussions.' This creates a clear contradiction because the contradicted text defines Confidential Information much more broadly than just documents related to discussions. The changed text now implies that other forms of Confidential Information, as previously defined, do not need to be returned or destroyed, directly contradicting the original intent and scope of the Confidential Information definition. This constitutes an Omissions - In Text Contradiction.
